{"step_by_step_thinking": "Achondroplasia is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. When both parents have achondroplasia, each parent has a 50% chance of passing on the mutated FGFR3 gene to their offspring. The possible genotypes of the offspring are: 
- 25% chance of being homozygous for the mutated gene (homozygous achondroplasia), which is usually incompatible with life due to respiratory insufficiency and neurologic deficits.
- 50% chance of being heterozygous (carrying one mutated gene), which results in achondroplasia.
- 25% chance of being homozygous normal (unaffected).
Therefore, the chance of having an unaffected baby (homozygous normal) is 25%. This is supported by Document [3], which explicitly states: 'When both parents are affected, their offspring have a 1 in 4 (25%) chance of having normal stature.'", "answer_choice": "B"}
